groups.question_title: What did the students say about each of the following volcanoes for their presentation?
groups.desc:
Questions 21-25
What did the students say about each of the following volcanoes for their presentation?
Choose FIVE answers from the box and write the correct letter, A-G, next to Questions 21-25.

list.title: F
list.content: no need to talk about it

groups.desc:
Questions 26-30
Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.

questions.title: In Erica's view, what is wrong with Ian's last presentation?

options.title: A
options.content: He did not read loud enough.

options.title: B
options.content: He did not understand the main reason.

options.title: C
options.content: He did not provide enough evidence.

questions.title: What materials do the students agree to add to their presentation?
By using extinct and dormant volcanoes, the students try to

options.title: A
options.content: distinguish between scientific and popular terms.

options.title: B
options.content: distinguish between two different types of volcanoes.

options.title: C
options.content: distinguish whether an eruption is about to happen.

questions.title: What did the professor say about Erica's last presentation?

options.title: A
options.content: She carried out an in-depth discussion with other students.

options.title: B
options.content: She did not provide sufficient data.

options.title: C
options.content: She did not fully develop a personal point.

questions.title: The students both agree that volcanoes

options.title: B
options.content: cannot help to reshape the landscape.

options.title: C
options.content: are not fully recognised for their values.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Hey Ian, how far along are you on preparing the presentation on volcanoes next week?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Not much.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Maybe we can straighten things out first?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Sure.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I decided to start with shield volcano.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: The name derives from its similarity in shape to a warrior's shield lying face up.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: The Hawaiian shield volcanoes are the most famous examples.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Professor Joan mentioned that many scholars have already done quite a lot of research in this field.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: We could retell conclusions from published papers.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I'm not sure.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I mean, is that even allowed?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I know we don't have to use first-hand data we collect, but copying others?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Come on, it's not like we are using their findings as our own.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: The professor said it would be okay as long as we cited our sources.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Oh, okay.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Do you have any ideas about stratovolcanoes?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Stratovolcanoes, also known as composite volcanoes, develop over many thousands of years through repeated eruptions and other volcanic activity.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But the current information I have at hand is only very vague.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Fuller descriptions are needed for this.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I'm sure you can find them.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Alright then.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Leave that to me.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Have you read anything about rhyolite caldera complexes?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Yeah, they are the most explosive volcanoes on Earth and don't even look like volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: The origin of these volcanoes, like Yellowstone, is associated with a hotspot.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: You've got most of the information correct.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Unfortunately, the last bit you mentioned is wrong.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: The origin of this type of volcano remains a mystery.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: The hotspot origin theory doesn't work for most other rhyolite calderas.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Right.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I'll change that part.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: What about the position on monogenetic fields?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: It's shocking to see the photos of these immense volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: There are numerous monogenetic fields in the American southwest and Mexico.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But I got all the information from a website without specifying its source.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: We'll have to examine the content again later.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Okay.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I can help with that.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Lastly, we have cinder cones.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: What do you have on that?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Well, this type of volcano is usually tiny to the other volcanoes and thus sounds less attractive.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Maybe we should leave it out; it seems pretty unimportant to me.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I agree.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: It's impossible to cover everything.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Erica, can you work on the delivery part?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I don't want to do it anymore.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I'm okay with that.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But why is that?

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I was marked so low for the last presentation and didn't even have a clue why.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Maybe it's because I didn't include enough data on it.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Well, I'm not sure about that.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: I think maybe you did not prepare the topic well enough.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: You tried to explain the contributing factor forming the Ring of Fire in the Pacific Ocean, but you just read everything out loud without fully comprehending it yourself.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Right.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: That must have been it.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: We also have to look for supporting materials to illustrate the formation of different volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: What about documentaries?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: They can be an excellent way to illustrate the volcanic eruption, but that might be too long for our presentation.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: We've only got 20 minutes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Have you considered adding photos to our handouts?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: That's an option.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But people may focus on these photos rather than paying attention to what we have to say.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Maybe we can search online for part of a film about volcanoes and add it to the end of our presentation.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: That would be better.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: So how should we start our presentation?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Maybe we can first introduce some terms like extinct volcanoes and dormant volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Why is that?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Even though they are widely-accepted terms among ordinary people, academia wouldn't accept them because there are no set definitions.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: For example, an extinct volcano would be one nobody expects to erupt again.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But there have been several eruptions from widely-acknowledged 'extinct' volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: That's an excellent way to start our presentation.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: What did the professor say about your last presentation?

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: There is still room for improvement.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: She said that I had an in-depth analysis and provided enough statistics, but I didn't carry on and discuss my take on it.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: Right.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: So maybe we could also discuss the impact volcanoes have on humans and our planet.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: Yeah.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: There's little doubt that volcanoes have been instrumental in wiping out countless species during major extinction events.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: But volcanic activity also shapes new habitats, enriching the soil with nutrients that allow life to thrive.

transcript.speaker: Erica
transcript.en: People haven't realised that there are abundant mineral resources waiting to be exploited in volcanoes.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: I agree.

transcript.speaker: Ian
transcript.en: So now we can...

21.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 shield volcanoes。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到介绍火山的信息，随后才是评论性信息。原文提及“我们可以复述已发表论文的结论”，对应 B 选项“重复其他人的研究”。随后女生提到“不需要用第一手的数据”，与 D 选项表述相悖。注意，配对题的难点并不只是能否听懂，更要培养结合语境理解具象化表达的能力。

22.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 stratovolcanoes。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到介绍火山的信息，随后才是评论性信息。原文提及“需要更全面的描述”，对应 E 选项“需要更多细节”。

23.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 rhyolite caldera complexes。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到介绍火山的信息，随后才是评论性信息。原文提及“不幸的是，你最后提到的部分是错误的”，对应 C 选项“不准确的信息”。

24.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 monogenetic fields。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到介绍火山的信息，然后才是评论性信息。女生最后提到“我们之后还要再检查一下内容”，对应 G 选项“再次检查”。

25.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 cinder cones。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到介绍火山的信息，然后才是评论性信息。男生最后提到“也许我们应该将其去掉”，对应 F 选项“没必要谈及”。

26.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 Ian's last presentation，条件为 Erica、wrong。
题目解析：先听到定位信息 last presentation 原词重现，接着男生 Ian 提到“也许是因为我没有提供足够的数据”，对应 C 选项，但注意题目要求的是女生的态度，因此需要继续往下听。随后女生提到“我不确定”，其实就是在委婉地否定该信息。然后她指出是因为“你没有完全理解”，对应 B 选项，因此 B 选项为正确答案。注意，女生还提到“大声地读出”，但并非 A 选项的声音不够大。

27.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 materials，条件为 agree to add。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着先听到男生提到 A 选项 documentaries，但随后女生说其太长了，否定了男生的建议。随后男生又提及了 B 选项，女生再次进行否定“人们可能会盯着照片而不去听我们说的话”。最后听到“在网上找关于火山的电影片段”，男生也表示同意，因此 C 选项为正确答案。

28.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 extinct and dormant volcanoes。
题目解析：定位词原词重现，接着女生提到“尽管大众普遍接受这些术语，但学术界并不认可，因为它们没有准确的定义”，对应 A 选项。女生之后提到的例子都是这一观点的佐证。她后续还提到了死火山，但并未说明其与休眠火山的区别，与 B 选项不符。C 选项在原文也仅出现“喷发”一词，属于信息不完整，可以排除。

29.analysis:
听前预测：定位词为 Erica's last presentation，条件为 the professor say。
题目解析：定位词通过男生提问的方式给出，接着就是女生的回答。首先听到“深入的分析”，对应 A 选项的部分信息，但后面并未提到与其他学生讨论。随后听到了选项的 data 被替换为 statistics，但其他信息与 B 选项对不上。最后听到“我没有继续讨论我的看法”，对应 C 选项。

30.analysis:
听前预测：条件为 both agree。
题目解析：A 选项中的“灾难性的后果”在原文被具象化替换为“消灭无数物种”，但原文表达的是肯定这一事实，而非 A 选项的否定。B 选项与原文的表述也相悖。C 选项在原文被替换为“人们没有意识到丰富的矿产资源”，表达完整且态度一致，因此 C 选项为正确答案。
